'''Barrie-Jon Mather''' (born 15 January 1973) is an English former [[rugby league]] and [[rugby union]] player. A [[List of dual-code rugby internationals|dual-code international]] player, he was the first [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] rugby league player to be capped by [[England national rugby union team|England]] at rugby union.

He was the [[NSWRL]]'s general manager of football until October 2020, when he was made redundant.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/former-test-cricketer-clark-moved-on-as-axe-falls-on-nswrl-officials-20201014-p5654w.html|title = Former Test cricketer Clark moved on as axe falls on NSWRL officials|date = 14 October 2020}}</ref>

==Early years==
Mather was born in [[Ince-in-Makerfield]], a suburb of [[Wigan]], [[Lancashire]], on 15 January 1973. He moved to [[Blackpool]] at a young age when his father took up a teaching position in [[Lytham St Annes]],<ref>{{cite news|last1=Glover|first1=Tim|title=From Wigan to Twickers First Night: Barrie-Jon Mather|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/from-wigan-to-twickers-first-night-barriejon-mather-1182002.html|website=The Independent|access-date=5 June 2015|date=1 November 1998}}</ref> and attended [[Arnold School]], where he quickly developed as a rugby union player. In 1991 he was selected in the England Schools rugby union team which lost a Grand Slam against Wales. Other notable players in that side were future World Cup winners [[Matt Dawson]] and [[Richard Hill (rugby union, born 1973)|Richard Hill]].

==Club rugby ==
===Wigan===

Despite his school boy success, Mather decided that his future lay in rugby league and eventually signed for the [[Wigan Warriors]] in 1992. He played at {{rlp|ce}} in the 2–33 defeat by [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] in the [[1993–94 League Cup (rugby league)|1993–94 Regal Trophy]] Final at [[Elland Road]], [[Leeds]] on 22 January 1994. He saw domestic success with Wigan and was a member of the [[Challenge Cup]]-winning side of 1994 as well as a member of the side that defeated the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in the [[1994 World Club Challenge]] at Brisbane's [[Queensland Sport and Athletics Centre|ANZ Stadium]]. Relations between the Mather and Wigan turned sour when Mather claimed his contract was null and void because of the circumstances in which it was signed.<ref>[http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_qn4158/is_19951119/ai_n14018844 Mather Tries to Break Free]</ref> Mather eventually took Wigan to court<ref>[http://archive.thisislancashire.co.uk/1996/1/20/860758.html Mather Takes Wigan to Court] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060227234956/http://archive.thisislancashire.co.uk/1996/1/20/860758.html|date=27 February 2006 }}</ref> when they refused to release his registration without a transfer fee following him signing a contract with [[WA Reds|Western Reds]] in Australia. He was unsuccessful in his challenge.<ref>[https://archive.today/20120709092109/http://archive.theboltonnews.co.uk/1996/1/26/860356.html Mather Loses Release Case]</ref>

===Western Reds===

Mather played a summer stint for the [[WA Reds|Western Reds]] in 1995 while at Wigan and fell in love with the Australian way of life. He eventually signed a three-year deal with the club but was unable to complete the move as he claimed to be a free agent but Wigan retained his registration. {{Citation needed|date=February 2016}}

Mather was selected for [[England national rugby league team|England]] in the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup|1995 World Cup]] Final on the reserve bench but did not play as [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] won the match and retained the Cup.

After two mixed seasons in the ARL and Super League in which he scored 5 tries for the club, the Western Reds suffered financial difficulties and Mather was released. {{Citation needed|date=February 2016}}

Mather is the only player to win [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] caps whilst at the [[Western Reds]].

===Castleford Tigers===

Mather signed for [[Castleford Tigers]] in October 1997 following the financial problems of the Western Reds. He stated that [[Stuart Raper]] had spoken to him whilst out in Perth for the [[World Club Challenge]] match and convinced him Castleford was the club for him.<ref>[http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/programmes/letter_from_america/15939.stm Mather signs for Cas]</ref> He played for Castleford for just one season before making a big money switch to rugby union.

===Sale===

Mather joined [[Sale Sharks]] in 1998 on a two-year deal mainly funded by the [[Rugby Football Union|RFU]]. It was the beginning of a period of recruitment from rugby league in which the RFU sought to bring some of the best talents to play for England. Mather did not have the best of times with Sale and played for the club until his contract release in 2000. During that time Sale struggled to make an impact in the [[Guinness Premiership|Premiership]], and Mather never lived up to the hype that surrounded his transfer.

===Back at Castleford===

Mather returned to [[Castleford Tigers]] in 2000<ref>[http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_qn4161/is_20000326/ai_n14503286 Mather rejoins Tigers]</ref> and in his first season back in [[Super League]] he made 16 appearances, scoring 7 tries. An injury hit 2001 restricted him to just 8 appearances in which he scored 2 tries following a full shoulder reconstruction. In 2002 Mather made 21 appearances for the Tigers and scored 7 tries for them. However towards the end of the 2002's [[Super League VII]], Mather announced that he would retire from rugby league at the end of the season.<ref>[http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/2261198.stm BJ calls in Quits]</ref>

===Return to union===

Following his retirement from rugby league, Mather returned to rugby union signing a one-year deal with [[Coventry R.F.C.|Coventry]] in National One. However his season was cut short when he broke his wrist in a game against Orrell and he was released at the end of the year.<ref>[http://www.coventrytelegraph.net/coventry-warwickshire-sport/rugby-union/coventry-rfc/2003/01/24/barrie-jon-s-season-over-92746-12565975/ Mather Season Over] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111003141847/http://www.coventrytelegraph.net/coventry-warwickshire-sport/rugby-union/coventry-rfc/2003/01/24/barrie-jon-s-season-over-92746-12565975|date=3 October 2011 }}</ref>

===Japan===

In 2003 it was announced Mather had signed a 12-month deal to play for [[Kubota Spears|Kubota]] in Japan.<ref>[http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/2771245.stm Mather Heads for Japan]</ref> Following his time there Mather retired from professional rugby.

==International rugby==

Mather represented [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] between 1994 and 1996 making 3 appearances. He was also selected for [[England national rugby league team|England]] for the 1995 World Cup where he made 3(2?) appearances including playing in the opening game against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]].

Despite struggling for form with his club Sale, Mather was a surprise call into [[Clive Woodward]]'s [[England national rugby union team|England team]] to face [[Wales national rugby union team|Wales]] in a [[Grand Slam (rugby union)|Grand Slam]] decider on 11 April 1999 at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]]. In doing so, he became the first player to represent England at rugby union after having also already played rugby league internationally. England looked to have the game and the Grand Slam won, but a sensational individual effort from [[Scott Gibbs]] sent England crashing to a 32–31 defeat and the [[Five Nations Championship]] went to [[Scotland national rugby union team|Scotland]]. Mather did not play again for England.

==Coaching and management==

Following his retirement after a 12 months spell in Japan, Mather was appointed coach of Blackpool RFC in the North Lancs One, following this he became Head Coach at Heaton Moor Rugby. In 2006 Mather was appointed Academy Coach at [[Guinness Premiership]] side [[London Irish RFC|London Irish]].<ref>[https://archive.today/20130420224832/http://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/sport/rugby_union/s/220/220148_mather_joins_exiles_as_academy_coach.html Mather Appointed Academy Coach]</ref>

On 11 June 2009 it was announced Mather would become the [[Rugby Football League|RFLs]] Head of Player Development on 1 July 2009.<ref>[http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_5375049,00.html Mather Appointed RFLs Head of Player Development]</ref> As part of his role he was also the England team manager.  In January 2014 it was announced Mather would become the General Manager, Football of the NSWRL based in Australia, starting in February 2014.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.totalrl.com/bj-mather-heads|title=BJ Mather heads down under | Rugby League News, Forum & Features|publisher=Totalrl.com|date=2014-01-14|access-date=2016-07-20}}</ref> He was made redundant from the role in October 2020.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/former-test-cricketer-clark-moved-on-as-axe-falls-on-nswrl-officials-20201014-p5654w.html|title = Former Test cricketer Clark moved on as axe falls on NSWRL officials|date = 14 October 2020}}</ref>
On 17 December 2023 it was announced that he would take charge of Fiji following their successful run in the RWC 2023.

In August 2025 Mather was appointed Director of Performance of the [[New South Wales Waratahs]] in the [[Super Rugby]],<ref>{{cite web |date=1 August 2025 |title=NSW Waratahs Appoint New Director of Performance |url=https://www.rugby.com.au/news/nsw-waratahs-appoint-new-director-of-performance-202581 |website=[[Rugby.com.au]] |access-date=4 August 2025 }}</ref> replacing [[Simon Raiwalui]].
